--- Detailed Conversation Log ---

[00:00] [Narrator]: On April 6, 2025 in Detroit, Michigan, police located a male in a vehicle suspected of murdering three people, one of them being a nine-year-old girl whose bodies were found in a burning vehicle on Detroit's west side. When a traffic stop was initiated, the suspect immediately took off, leading police on a high-speed pursuit on the busy streets of Detroit.
[00:22] Police Officer (Voice 2): 75. Now he's taking it. I'm going to try and pit this guy as soon as I can. We're coming up to Fort and Shafer intersection now. 50 miles an hour. Almost crashed. Wrong way on Fort, wrong way on Fort. West on Fort, west on Fort. Slamming on the brakes. Couldn't make it. He's on a flat. He's on a flat. Hitting the turn around to get back on Fort. Uh going the wrong way in the other direction, heading back towards Shafer. Lost it. Crashed in the Boulevard.
[00:48] [Narrator]: 50. 50. He's right here. You will get tased if you don't. I got him. I got him. I'll take him over to my car. Okay. I'm ready. Get him up. This suspect Edward Reading was charged with carrying concealed weapons, four counts of felony firearm possession, firearm possession by a prohibited person, assaulting, resisting or obstructing a police officer, and fourth degree fleeing a police officer in a vehicle. Reports indicate the murder victims were the mother, daughter, and sister of Reading's ex-girlfriend. Reading was released from prison on parole just 10 months before the murders for carjacking, armed robbery, and felony firearm possession. Due to the case still being investigated by the ATF, Reading has not yet been sentenced for his alleged connection to the murders or the high-speed pursuit. While police were able to quickly apprehend Reading, this next case shows police having difficulty stopping a suspect in yet another high-speed pursuit in New Mexico when a pit maneuver is executed, but the suspect is still able to get away and continue fleeing at increasingly high speeds.
[01:02] [Narrator]: that car is traveling wrong way on. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. 75. You're going to get tased if you don't. I got him. I got him. I'll take him over to my car. Okay. I'm ready. Get him up. This suspect Edward Reading was charged with carrying concealed weapons, four counts of felony firearm possession, firearm possession by a prohibited person, assaulting, resisting or obstructing a police officer, and fourth degree fleeing a police officer in a vehicle. Reports indicate the murder victims were the mother, daughter, and sister of Reading's ex-girlfriend. Reading was released from prison on parole just 10 months before the murders for carjacking, armed robbery, and felony firearm possession. Due to the case still being investigated by the ATF, Reading has not yet been sentenced for his alleged connection to the murders or the high-speed pursuit. While police were able to quickly apprehend Reading, this next case shows police having difficulty stopping a suspect in yet another high-speed pursuit in New Mexico when a pit maneuver is executed, but the suspect is still able to get away and continue fleeing at increasingly high speeds.

You don't know this guy. He's driving like that. You said the cops are chasing you and then you said the cops spiked the car? And why didn't you ask him? I mean, a reasonable person seems to say like, hey, let me out of this car. I don't know you. I want to get out. Why didn't you say that? [12:17] [Narrator]: This incident began due to no license plate being displayed on the suspect's vehicle. The suspect was charged with aggravated fleeing a law enforcement officer, resisting, obstructing, reckless driving, operating an unregistered motor vehicle, improper license plate, and a felony warrant. His warrant was likely due to his previous criminal offenses involving shooting at or from a motor vehicle, aggravated fleeing a law enforcement officer, a felonious probation violation, reckless driving, and tampering with evidence. The passenger that was in the vehicle was medically cleared and charged with resisting, obstructing. They are both innocent unless proven guilty in a court of law. Who actually owns a city street, the law or the criminal who thinks he's beyond the law? And there's a myth that police own the pace in a chase. We don't though. The suspect decides how reckless this gets. All we do is apply pressure in a way that forces a bad decision to crash into a better one. That's not a game, it's a gamble where you stack the odds with every tactical move. And the public might imagine a straight choice, stop the pursuit and save lives, but on the street, it's a lot messier. And stopping the pursuit does not make the danger disappear. It just moves it somewhere where you're not watching. It's like fixing one leak while the water pours in from somewhere else. And these pursuits only expose the limits of the badge. You can train, you can prepare, you can run the playbook perfectly and still fail to stop someone whose only plan is disaster. And that knowledge sits in the back of a cop's mind every time they hit the siren. And Detroit chases must be like a knife fight in a phone booth. You have tight grids, endless intersections, and bystanders just a few feet from disaster. But New Mexico flips the equation. Out there, the threat isn't proximity, it's velocity. Long open stretches of highway become runways for desperation where a suspect can rebuild speed no matter how many times you slow him down. And that laugh at the end tells you everything you need to know. It's not fear, it's familiarity. This isn't adrenaline, it's addiction, addiction to risk, to control, it's approving if only to themselves that they can spit in the face of authority and walk away. But every pursuit has a last chapter, and if the law doesn't write it, the physics of the road eventually will.
